title: Maple fudge
subtitle: Condensed milk fudge with pure maple syrup
excerpt: A no-fail fudge method using condensed milk and maple syrup. Cooked to soft ball stage, beaten until it loses its gloss, then poured into a tin to set. The maple flavour comes through clearly. Cut into small squares; it is very sweet and rich.
sourceNotes: Condensed milk fudge is a well-established British home confectionery method that simplifies the traditional Scottish fudge by using condensed milk to provide both the dairy and a portion of the sugar. Maple syrup as a flavouring agent in fudge is common in Canadian and North American home confectionery. This recipe applies the condensed milk method to the maple fudge flavour profile.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut into squares, layer with baking paper, and freeze in a sealed container for up to 3 months. Thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fudge keeps at room temperature in an airtight tin for up to 2 weeks. It firms further over the first day after making.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven; cooked on the hob to soft ball stage (115 degrees C)

glossaryTerms.definition: After cooking to temperature, fudge must be beaten to encourage small sugar crystals to form throughout the mixture. Beat with a wooden spoon until the mixture thickens, loses its gloss, and starts to look matte and grainy. This takes 5 to 10 minutes. Stop and pour immediately when it reaches this point: if you beat too long, the fudge will set in the pan. If it is still glossy when you pour it, the texture will be smooth and toffee-like rather than crumbly.

content.text: The condensed milk method is forgiving compared to traditional grain fudge, but the
still matters. Reaching soft ball stage (115 degrees C) and then beating until matte gives the characteristic crumbly fudge texture. A sugar thermometer removes the guesswork.

Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Put 397 g condensed milk, 100 ml maple syrup, and 100 g butter in a heavy-based saucepan. Stir over low heat until the butter melts and the ingredients are combined. Increase the heat to medium and cook, stirring constantly, until the mixture reaches 115 degrees C on a sugar thermometer (soft ball stage). This takes approximately 15 to 20 minutes. Remove from the heat and add 1 tsp vanilla.

Leave the pan to cool for 5 minutes, then beat vigorously with a wooden spoon. The mixture will thicken, lose its gloss, and take on a matte, slightly grainy appearance. This takes 5 to 10 minutes. Pour immediately into the lined tin and spread level. Leave to set at room temperature for 2 hours, then cut into small squares with a hot knife.

items.problem: Fudge is too soft and sticky after setting
items.solution: The mixture did not reach 115 degrees C, or the beating was not long enough. Under-beaten fudge can be returned to the pan, remelted, and brought back to temperature before beating again.

items.problem: Fudge is grainy and crumbles rather than cutting cleanly
items.solution: The mixture was beaten too long or was too cool when poured. Pour while still warm as soon as it turns matte. A hot knife (dipped in hot water and wiped dry) cuts more cleanly.
